questions.question: What is the primary purpose of the 'Helmet' middleware in Express?
options: It encrypts the entire Node.js server to prevent malware infections
It automatically patches vulnerabilities in NPM dependencies
It obscures the 'X-Powered-By' header and sets 15+ modern security HTTP headers (like Content Security Policy and Strict-Transport-Security) to defend against common attack vectors
It acts as a physical firewall layer that filters incoming traffic before it hits the Node.js instances
questions.answer: It obscures the 'X-Powered-By' header and sets 15+ modern security HTTP headers (like Content Security Policy and Strict-Transport-Security) to defend against common attack vectors
questions.explanation: Express provides zero security headers by default and openly advertises 'X-Powered-By: Express'. Helmet instantly fixes this by applying well-researched, hardened HTTP response headers the moment it is initialized as middleware.

questions.question: Why must you strictly configure CORS origins instead of using the default `app.use(cors())`?
options: Default CORS enables a 'wildcard' origin (*), which allows any malicious website on the internet to make authenticated API requests to your backend using a victim's browser session
Because default CORS blocks all traffic, including your own frontend React app
Default CORS slows down the server by adding massive HTTP response headers natively
It forces the browser to bypass HTTPS requirements and downgrade to HTTP automatically
questions.answer: Default CORS enables a 'wildcard' origin (*), which allows any malicious website on the internet to make authenticated API requests to your backend using a victim's browser session
questions.explanation: If your API is api.com and valid frontend is admin.com, configuring CORS strictly ensures browsers will block evil.com from making Fetch/XHR requests to api.com on behalf of an unwitting logged-in user.

questions.question: How do you defend your /login route from brute-force password guessing attacks?
options: By enforcing 20-character passwords
By encrypting the database with an AES-256 key automatically
By utilizing 'express-rate-limit' middleware to block an IP address temporarily if it attempts to log in too many times within a specific time window (e.g., max 5 attempts per hour)
By using JWTs exclusively
questions.answer: By utilizing 'express-rate-limit' middleware to block an IP address temporarily if it attempts to log in too many times within a specific time window (e.g., max 5 attempts per hour)
questions.explanation: Rate limiting is the only defense against automated credential stuffing. If an attacker's bot script tries 10,000 passwords a second, rate limiters detect the volume from that IP and reject all further requests with a 429 Too Many Requests status.

questions.question: How does a NoSQL Injection attack typically work against a MongoDB/Mongoose database?
options: An attacker injects standard SQL syntax like 'DROP TABLE users' into a text field, which Mongoose blindly executes globally
The attacker exploits server hardware vulnerabilities to read random memory addresses

The attacker structures their JSON payload to include MongoDB query operators (like { \"$gt\": \"\" }) instead of a string, tricking the query into evaluating as 'true' and bypassing authentication checks
The attacker uploads a malformed image file containing MongoDB binary blobs
questions.answer: The attacker structures their JSON payload to include MongoDB query operators (like { \"$gt\": \"\" }) instead of a string, tricking the query into evaluating as 'true' and bypassing authentication checks
questions.explanation: If a developer writes User.findOne(req.body), and the hacker sends { email: { $gt: '' }, password: { $gt: '' } }, the database interprets it as 'Find a user where email is greater than empty string', which always returns the first document (often an admin user) regardless of the password.

questions.question: What is the industry-standard way to manage sensitive production configuration data like Database URLs and JWT Secrets in a Node.js project?
options: Hardcode them globally in a config.js file and use Git encryption
Store them in the server's package.json dependencies object natively
Store them in a `.env` file loaded via the 'dotenv' module, and ensuring the `.env` file is heavily .gitignored so secrets are never pushed to a remote repository
Write them into a JWT token payload to retrieve on boot
questions.answer: Store them in a `.env` file loaded via the 'dotenv' module, and ensuring the `.env` file is heavily .gitignored so secrets are never pushed to a remote repository
questions.explanation: Secrets must exist uniquely on the production server (in environment variables) and never in source control. Storing them in code guarantees they will eventually be leaked or scraped by automated GitHub bots.
